1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum(

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" values will be grouped together. Use ``case_sensitive=True`` to disable this and instead treat "CA" and "ca" as separate groups. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive, environment) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive, environment) def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], case_sensitive: bool, environment: "Environment", ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: return ignore_case(expr(item)) return [ _GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in ( (key, list(group)) for key, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key=key_func) ) ]

For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1176 the ``city`` value of the group. 1177 1178 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1179 1180
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1181
  • {{ city }} 1182
      {% for user in items %} 1183
    • {{ user.name }} 1184 {% endfor %}
    1185
    • 1186 {% endfor %}
1187 1188 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1189 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1190 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1191 1192 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1193 1194
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1195
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1196 {% endfor %}
1197 1198 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1199 does not have the given attribute. 1200 1201 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1202 1203
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1204
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1205 {% endfor %}
1206 1207 Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is 1208 case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have 1209 the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if 1210 a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" 1211 values will be grouped together. Use ``case_sensitive=True`` to 1212 disable this and instead treat "CA" and "ca" as separate groups. 1213 1214 .. versionchanged:: 3.1 1215 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is 1216 case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do 1217 comparisons. 1218 1219 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1220 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1221 1222 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1223 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1224 """ 1225 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1226 1227 out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive, environment) 1228 return out 1229 1230 1231 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1232 async def do_groupby( 1233 environment: "Environment", 1234 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1235 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1236 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1237 case_sensitive: bool = False, 1238 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1239 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1240 1241 return _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive, environment) 1242 1243 1244 def _group_by( 1245 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1246 expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], 1247 case_sensitive: bool, 1248 environment: "Environment", 1249 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1250 if case_sensitive: 1251 key_func = expr 1252 else: 1253 1254 def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: 1255 return ignore_case(expr(item)) 1256 1257 return [ 1258 _GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values) 1259 for _, values in (

file.content:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" values will be grouped together. Use ``case_sensitive=True`` to disable this and instead treat "CA" and "ca" as separate groups. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive, environment) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive, environment) def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], case_sensitive: bool, environment: "Environment", ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: return ignore_case(expr(item)) return [ _GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in (

content.text: Now let's verify with a quick test and run the existing test suite for groupby.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}\n{% endfor %}')
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
print('---case sensitive---')
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=True) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}\n{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Both sync cases work as intended. Now let's check the async variant and run the existing test suite.
All tests pass. Let's quickly verify the async version too.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}\n{% endfor %}')
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"

content.text: Now let's run mypy/flake8-style checks if configured, and check CHANGES.rst for a convention to add an entry.

input.command: head -30 CHANGES.rst; echo ---; python -m mypy src/jinja2/filters.py 2>&1 | tail -30

content.content:
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
---
/usr/local/bin/python: No module named mypy

.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
---

""" expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) - ] + return _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive) @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore @@ -1222,12 +1232,31 @@ async def do_groupby( value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) - ] + return _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive) + + +def _group_by( + value: "t.Iterable[V]", + expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], + case_sensitive: bool, +) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": + if case_sensitive: + key_func = expr + else: + + def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: + return ignore_case(expr(item)) + + result = [] + + for _, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key=key_func): + values = list(values) + result.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) + + return result @pass_environment

content.text: Added a `case_sensitive` parameter (default `False`) to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, sharing logic via a new `_group_by` helper. When case-insensitive, items are sorted/grouped by a lowercased key but each group's `grouper` preserves the original case of the first item in that group — matching `sort()`'s default behavior. Verified with manual sync/async renders and the full test suite (837 passed). Diff written to `patch.txt`.
